metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the 'S1 non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack)' referenced in the Security section actually exists in the codebase (or will be introduced by S1 before S5 ships); if it does not, either define the gate inline in S5 or add an explicit startup check that refuses to start the proxy verifier when bound to a non-loopback address without the required config.
evidence: Plan text: 'a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack) — no new bind key'
Search of src/rebar/mcp_server.py for 'bind', 'allowlist', 'TLS': zero matches
Search of src/rebar/_config_schema.py for 'bind': zero matches
Search of entire repo for 'non_loopback_gate', 'TLS-at-edge': zero matches in src/
Ticket lookup for S1 returns: 'Ticket S1 not found'
ABSENCE: no non-loopback gate, allowlist-check, or TLS-at-edge acknowledgement mechanism exists in any MCP server code path
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack) — no new bind key' as a pre-existing codebase seam that covers S5's bind-security boundary. No such gate exists anywhere in `src/rebar/mcp_server.py`, `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, or any other file in `src/rebar/`. The S1 ticket also returns 'not found'. This exclusion deletes the work of specifying how non-loopback deployments are actually constrained, resting S5's security boundary on an unverifiable premise.
findings.location: Security section: 'a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack) — no new bind key'
scenarios: An operator deploys the MCP server on a non-loopback interface (e.g. 0.0.0.0) believing S1's gate protects them; since the gate does not exist, any client can reach the proxy-auth endpoint directly and bypass the intended proxy-only deployment model.
A downstream story or security audit finds the claimed gate does not exist and the S5 security model lacks its stated boundary condition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete location(s) where the operator prerequisite warning will live: e.g., a dedicated 'Trusted-proxy deployment checklist' subsection in the README/operator guide AND/OR a startup log WARNING emitted when the proxy strategy is active.
evidence: 'Operator prerequisite (documented, loud): the fronting proxy MUST overwrite/strip all client-supplied identity and forwarded headers before adding its own'
ABSENCE: no doc file, README section, or startup-warning mechanism is identified as the target for this 'loud' documentation
findings.finding: The 'operator prerequisite' (the fronting proxy MUST overwrite/strip all client-supplied identity and forwarded headers) is described as 'documented, loud' but the plan does not specify WHERE this warning will appear — no target doc, README section, or startup log message is named.
findings.location: ## Security — 'Operator prerequisite (documented, loud)'
scenarios: An operator deploys rebar behind a misconfigured proxy that does not strip client headers; the warning was never surfaced in any doc they consulted, leading to a header-injection vulnerability
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or 'What' bullet naming the target: 'a WARNING-level startup log line when `proxy` is in `auth_strategies` reminding operators that the fronting proxy must strip client-supplied forwarded headers; and a deployment-prerequisites subsection in docs/operators.md (or README Security section).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete propagation channel for the validated proxy identity from the ASGI middleware to the TokenVerifier-shaped component (e.g., ASGI `scope` dict key, a `contextvars.ContextVar`, or a sanitized internal header), and confirm that choice is compatible with how S2's composite invokes verifiers under FastMCP's async dispatch.
evidence: Plan: 'it validates the shared-secret header (constant-time) and, only if valid, extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal'
Plan: 'identity is trusted ONLY when the secret validates AND the header survived rebar\'s strip-then-repopulate'
Plan: 'The verifier turns that verified request-scoped identity into an AccessToken'

ABSENCE: no statement of whether propagation uses ASGI scope dict mutation, a Python contextvar, or an internal sanitized header — three structurally different approaches with different failure modes under FastMCP's async dispatch

Codebase pattern: existing FastAPI apps (review_bot/app.py, opcert_service/app.py) use request.state for intra-request side-channels, but the MCP stack (FastMCP over stdio) may not expose request.state at all
findings.finding: The mechanism by which the ASGI middleware propagates the validated proxy identity to the downstream TokenVerifier-shaped component is unspecified and internally inconsistent: the plan calls it a 'request-scoped principal' (implying a side-channel like an ASGI scope dict key or a contextvar) but also uses the phrase 'strip-then-repopulate' (implying the middleware re-injects a sanitized internal header). These are structurally different choices with different failure modes: an internal header may be stripped by FastMCP before the verifier sees it; a contextvar may not propagate across async task boundaries that S2's composite uses; an ASGI scope dict mutation requires both the middleware and the verifier to operate on the same scope object, which holds only if FastMCP passes the raw scope through. Without specifying the propagation channel, an implementer may choose an approach that silently breaks identity flow without any test catching it (because the unit tests exercise the middleware and verifier directly, not necessarily through FastMCP's actual request-dispatch chain).
findings.location: Approach & rationale / What section — 'request-scoped principal' + 'strip-then-repopulate' + 'The verifier turns that verified request-scoped identity into an AccessToken'
scenarios: Implementer chooses an internal header (e.g. X-Verified-Proxy-Identity): FastMCP's MCP layer strips unknown HTTP headers before dispatching to tool handlers, so the verifier never sees the identity → every proxy request is unauthenticated with no error

Implementer chooses a contextvar: if S2's composite dispatches verifiers in a new asyncio Task without copying the context, the contextvar is unset → verifier returns None → composite falls through to 401
ASGI scope dict mutation: works only if the verifier receives the same scope object, which requires that S2 passes scope through the composite interface — not specified in this plan or in S2
findings.suggested_fix: Choose an ASGI `scope` dict key (e.g., `scope['rebar.proxy_principal']`) written by the middleware and read by the verifier — this is the canonical ASGI pattern for intra-request state, requires no import of async-context machinery, and is visible to any downstream ASGI component operating on the same request scope object. State this explicitly in the Approach section and in the AC for the unit-test that exercises the integrated middleware→composite→verifier chain.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The middleware runs before the MCP app' but does not verify or specify how an ASGI middleware is attached to a FastMCP server instance — the current codebase uses `FastMCP.run()` (stdio mode) and there is no established seam in the repo for wrapping FastMCP with ASGI middleware. The FastMCP HTTP ASGI surface attachment mechanism is unverified.
findings.location: What section: 'The middleware runs before the MCP app'; Integration test AC: 'httpx ASGITransport'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the plan which mechanism the middleware uses to pass the validated proxy identity to the verifier (e.g., 'store in ASGI `request.state.proxy_principal`; the verifier reads `request.state` and never reads raw headers'), so the executor makes no open-ended design decision on a security-critical channel.
evidence: 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal' — 'request-scoped' is hinted but the mechanism (ASGI state, contextvar, synthetic internal-only header) is not stated

Security section: 'identity is trusted ONLY when the secret validates AND the header survived rebar's strip-then-repopulate' — 'strip-then-repopulate' implies a concrete in-process channel but leaves the channel unspecified
An executor choosing a sanitized header (e.g., X-Rebar-Proxy-Identity) vs. ASGI state changes both testability and the spoofing surface differently
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the internal channel by which the ASGI middleware communicates the validated proxy identity to the verifier component (e.g., ASGI `request.state`, a context variable, or a sanitized synthetic header). For a security-critical path, this is a design decision the executor must make without guidance — choosing wrongly (e.g., a synthetic header that is also readable externally) could re-open the spoofing surface.
findings.location: What section / Approach & rationale — 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal'
scenarios: Executor uses a synthetic forwarded header as the IPC channel, which a co-located service could inject if the middleware is not the outermost layer
Executor uses a module-level contextvar that is not properly scoped per-request in async contexts, leaking identity across concurrent requests
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach section: 'The middleware stores the validated identity in `request.state.proxy_principal` (an ASGI-standard per-request slot); the verifier reads only that slot and never re-reads raw request headers, ensuring no raw-header path survives into the verifier.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the request-scoped principal storage mechanism explicitly (e.g., ASGI `scope` dict key or `contextvars.ContextVar`) so implementors cannot accidentally use a global/module-level variable that would cause cross-request identity contamination under concurrency.
evidence: 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal' — storage mechanism not named
ASGI apps handle many concurrent requests; a global/module-level store would mix identities across concurrent requests
No AC or implementation note specifies contextvar vs ASGI scope dict vs any other mechanism
findings.finding: The mechanism for storing the 'request-scoped principal' (extracted by the middleware, consumed by the verifier) is unspecified. In concurrent async ASGI code, using a module-level variable or any non-request-scoped store would cause cross-request identity contamination — a critical security flaw. The plan must specify the storage mechanism (ASGI scope dict or contextvars.ContextVar).
findings.location: What section: 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal'
scenarios: Concurrent requests arrive: request A sets a valid proxy identity; request B has no secret; if stored globally, request B inherits request A's identity — authentication bypass
Under async I/O, a single 'request' may yield between the middleware write and the verifier read — a ContextVar is required, not a simple global
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Approach section: 'The middleware stores the verified principal in the ASGI `scope` dict under a namespaced key (e.g., `scope["rebar.proxy_principal"]`); the verifier reads from the same key. This is safe under concurrent async dispatch because each request has its own `scope` dict.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and name the specific FastMCP API surface (e.g. `mcp.FastMCP.get_asgi_app()`, a Starlette router, or a lower-level `StreamableHTTPServerTransport`-based callable) that the ASGI middleware wraps, verify it exists in mcp>=1.9, and state in the plan how the production server activates HTTP transport so the middleware actually executes.
evidence: Plan: 'The middleware runs before the MCP app'
Plan: 'Integration test (httpx ASGITransport) asserts an authenticated proxy request'
mcp_server.py line 314: build_server().run() — no transport argument, stdio-only
UNRESOLVED: get_asgi_app, http_app, Middleware, StreamableHTTPSessionManager on mcp.server.fastmcp
VERIFIED: mcp.server.streamable_http.StreamableHTTPServerTransport EXISTS — so HTTP transport exists in the mcp SDK, but the FastMCP high-level ASGI accessor is unverified
Evidence class: Partially-verified (HTTP transport capability confirmed, specific ASGI-app accessor unconfirmed)
findings.finding: The plan asserts an ASGI middleware runs 'before the MCP app' and integration tests use httpx.ASGITransport, but the existing server is stdio-only (build_server().run() with no transport argument). The plan never names the FastMCP API surface used to obtain the wrappable ASGI callable (e.g. a hypothetical mcp.get_asgi_app() or http_app() method), nor does it specify that the server must be switched to or extended with an HTTP/ASGI transport for the middleware to execute. The specific FastMCP method exposing the ASGI app is unverified — probing common names (get_asgi_app, http_app, Middleware, StreamableHTTPSessionManager) all returned UNRESOLVED.
findings.location: What section ('The middleware runs before the MCP app') and Integration test AC ('httpx ASGITransport')
scenarios: If FastMCP has no public get_asgi_app() or equivalent, the implementer must construct the ASGI callable from lower-level mcp transport primitives — adding significant implementation risk not reflected in the plan's scope.
If the server stays stdio-only in production, the ASGI middleware is dead code and the proxy auth feature silently never executes.
Integration tests using httpx.ASGITransport will fail at test-authoring time if no ASGI callable can be extracted from FastMCP.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the middleware's valid-secret behavior between 'What' and 'Security': explicitly specify in 'What' that the middleware strips all X-Forwarded-* and identity/secret headers from the request unconditionally, then re-injects only the verified identity into a request-scoped principal (not back onto headers) — and add an AC that tests this strip occurs even when the secret is valid.
evidence: What: 'it validates the shared-secret header... and, only if valid, extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal; otherwise it strips it. It strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family...'
Security: 'identity is trusted ONLY when the secret validates AND the header survived rebar's strip-then-repopulate'

The 'What' section describes stripping only in the 'otherwise' (invalid) branch; 'strip-then-repopulate' in Security implies stripping happens first for ALL requests, then re-injection for valid ones — these are different behaviors.
No AC covers the valid-secret case's header sanitization (only AC3 covers the invalid-secret strip)
findings.finding: The 'What' section only describes stripping identity/forwarded headers for the *invalid-secret* path, while the Security section uses 'strip-then-repopulate' language implying headers are also stripped (after extraction) in the *valid-secret* path. An implementer reading only 'What' would skip the post-extraction strip, leaving raw identity headers forwarded downstream.
findings.location: What section (middleware bullet) vs Security section (3rd hard requirement bullet)
scenarios: Implementer follows 'What' literally: raw X-Forwarded-User header is forwarded downstream for valid-secret requests, enabling downstream confusion or re-injection.
Security section's 'strip-then-repopulate' intent is never tested because no AC verifies it.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' middleware bullet, change 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal; otherwise it strips it. It strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family...' to 'unconditionally strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family plus the configured identity and secret headers; if the secret validated, it re-injects the verified identity as a request-scoped principal (not as a header).' Add an AC: 'Unit test asserts that even for a valid-secret request, the raw X-Forwarded-User and X-Forwarded-* headers are removed from the forwarded request (strip-then-repopulate; headers are never passed through as-is).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the middleware/verifier fallback for the case where the proxy secret is valid but the identity header is absent or empty (e.g. 'verifier returns None → composite falls through' vs '401 immediately') and add an AC negative-control for this path.
evidence: Plan text: 'validates the shared-secret header … and, only if valid, extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal' — does not specify what happens when extraction yields empty/absent

Plan text: 'If no verified proxy identity is present it returns None' — the verifier returns None, but the middleware's behavior in this state (does it pass the request through with no principal, or 401 immediately?) is unspecified
AC tests cover: valid secret + identity → 200; no secret → 401; but no AC covers: valid secret + missing identity header
findings.finding: The middleware's behavior when the shared secret IS valid but the identity header (`x-forwarded-user`) is absent or empty is unspecified — the plan only describes two paths: (1) valid secret + identity header present → trust identity, and (2) invalid/missing secret → strip and deny. The fallback decision rule for valid-secret-but-no-identity is a structural gap in the processing protocol.
findings.location: What section: middleware and verifier descriptions; Security section: 'identity is trusted ONLY when the secret validates AND the header survived rebar's strip-then-repopulate'
scenarios: A misconfigured proxy sends the correct secret but omits the identity header; the middleware passes the request through with no principal; the composite falls through to the next verifier (e.g. bearer), potentially granting access via a different path than intended
An implementor interpreting 'returns None' as 'middleware should 401 immediately on missing identity' may short-circuit the composite, breaking the `proxy,static` fallthrough AC

findings.finding: The unit test AC for the shared-secret check specifies 'without the secret' but does not explicitly require a test for a *wrong* (present but invalid) secret value. For a constant-time-compare gate, these are distinct cases: an absent header vs. a header with a wrong value. Failing to explicitly cover the wrong-value case means the test suite could pass even if the constant-time compare is misconfigured (e.g., always-truthy on non-empty string).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (unit test for middleware/verifier)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2: 'with the secret header present but containing an incorrect value, the identity header is also stripped and the request returns 401 — confirming the constant-time compare rejects wrong values, not just absent ones.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (unit or integration) asserting that a request with a valid shared-secret header but an absent or empty identity header is denied (401/None from verifier) and emits a detectable diagnostic, to cover the proxy-misconfiguration failure path.
evidence: AC2 covers: valid secret + present identity → AccessToken; no secret → stripped + 401. It does not cover: valid secret + absent/empty identity header.
Plan prose states 'If no verified proxy identity is present it returns None' but this path has no corresponding test AC.
This is a realistic deployment failure mode: a correctly-configured proxy secret with a misconfigured identity-header name.
findings.finding: The testing plan has no explicit AC covering the edge case where the shared-secret header IS valid but the identity header is absent or empty — a realistic proxy misconfiguration scenario that produces a distinct failure path (verifier returns None → composite denies).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (unit test) and AC6 (integration test)
scenarios: Operator deploys with correct shared secret but misconfigures `auth_proxy_identity_header` (e.g., wrong header name). Secret validates, identity header is never present, request silently falls through to 401 with no diagnostic. Without a test, this path is unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 or add a new AC: 'Unit test asserts: when the shared-secret header is valid but the configured identity header is absent (or empty-valued), the verifier returns None and the request is unauthenticated (401); observable oracle: 401 response, no AccessToken minted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` and `docs/env-vars.md` as consumers requiring update (or extend `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` to cover the new auth env-read helper in `_mcp_auth.py`) so the env-registry drift gate stays green.
evidence: scripts/gen_env_registry.py:32: `DEFAULT_SCAN_ROOT = REPO_ROOT / 'src' / 'rebar'`
scripts/gen_env_registry.py:37-47: KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS lists specific helper names only; indirect env reads are not captured
Plan 'What': 'auth_proxy_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the proxy shared secret — no plaintext secret in config)' — the actual env var read is determined at runtime by config, not statically
.github/workflows/test.yml:260: `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py`
ABSENCE: `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` is not named in plan's Scope or AC
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` is an unenumerated consumer: it statically scans `src/rebar` for env-var reads and generates `docs/env-vars.md`, gated by a CI drift check. The new `_mcp_auth.py` reads an env var whose *name* is itself supplied via config (`auth_proxy_secret_env`), which is an indirected read the scanner's static patterns cannot capture. The resulting `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET` (or whatever the operator names it) will not appear in `docs/env-vars.md`, but more critically, the env var value read *by* `auth_proxy_secret_env` may also be absent. The plan does not name this script or `docs/env-vars.md` as edit targets.
findings.location: What section — `auth_proxy_secret_env` config key; Scope section
scenarios: The `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_*` config env vars (e.g. `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET_ENV`) may or may not be caught by static scan depending on how they are read in `_mcp_auth.py`; if read via `os.environ.get(config.auth_proxy_secret_env)` the inner secret var is invisible to the scanner.
CI drift gate on `docs/env-vars.md` could fail or silently omit security-relevant env vars from the registry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/config.md` to the plan's edit-target list and update the `# MCP server gates` section with the new `mcp.auth_proxy_*` keys (or explicitly note this is deferred to S7 with a reference).
evidence: docs/config.md:135-138: `# MCP server gates` section manually lists `mcp.readonly`, `mcp.allow_llm`, `mcp.allow_jira_sync`
Plan 'What': 'Adds the `auth_proxy_*` fields to `McpConfig`/`_SECTIONS['mcp']`'
ABSENCE: `docs/config.md` not named in plan Scope, AC, or edit targets
findings.finding: `docs/config.md` manually documents `mcp.*` config keys (confirmed at line 135–138) but is not named as an edit target. Adding four new `mcp.auth_proxy_*` keys without updating this file leaves the operator-facing config reference stale.
findings.location: What section — `auth_proxy_*` config fields; Scope section
scenarios: Operator reads `docs/config.md` to configure trusted-proxy mode and finds no reference to `mcp.auth_proxy_*` keys; no discoverability without the reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or verify the CVE citations (CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484): look up real CVE IDs for oauth2-proxy trusted-header spoofing vulnerabilities, or restate the threat model without relying on specific CVE numbers that cannot be confirmed.
evidence: Plan text: 'This is the documented mitigation for the forwarded-header-spoofing class (oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484)'
CVE-2026-40575 has a year (2026) in the future — it cannot be a real assigned CVE
Neither CVE ID appears anywhere in the repo codebase or documentation
findings.finding: The plan cites 'oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484' as the prior-art/risk basis for the header-stripping security requirement. CVE-2026-40575 is future-dated (year 2026) and cannot be a published CVE; neither identifier appears anywhere in the repo or any verifiable reference. Fabricated or unverifiable CVE numbers as a security justification is an unverified capability/evidence assertion that undermines the rationale.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section — 'This is the documented mitigation for the forwarded-header-spoofing class (oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484)'
scenarios: A reviewer or auditor tries to look up these CVEs to validate the security posture and finds they do not exist, undermining trust in the plan's security reasoning.
The real vulnerability motivating the design is different from what's described, leading to an incomplete mitigation.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the CVE numbers and replace with a factual description of the threat: e.g. 'This is the documented mitigation for forwarded-header spoofing — a class where a client can inject X-Forwarded-User directly if the upstream proxy does not strip it, bypassing authentication.' Reference oauth2-proxy's own documented secure-header pattern instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit requirement (Security section + AC) that the proxy shared-secret header value MUST be redacted/suppressed from all request logs and traces; specify that the middleware must not pass the raw secret value to any logging call.
evidence: Plan specifies constant-time compare and stripping of the secret header, but is silent on log redaction.
ABSENCE: no 'redact' / 'do not log' / 'sanitize headers' requirement anywhere in the Security section or Acceptance Criteria.
The secret is a bearer credential in an HTTP header — standard ASGI middleware (e.g., uvicorn's access log) can log all headers unless explicitly suppressed.
findings.finding: The plan does not require that the proxy shared-secret header value be redacted from ASGI access logs or debug-level request logging. If the MCP server runs with header-logging enabled (a common debug pattern), the shared secret travels in plaintext log lines — a plaintext-secret-on-boundary-path gap.
findings.location: Security section / middleware spec — no mention of log redaction
scenarios: A developer enables debug-level ASGI logging to diagnose a proxy configuration issue; the shared secret appears in log files, which may be shipped to a log aggregator (Datadog, Splunk, CloudWatch) accessible to a broader audience than the server operator.
A CI/CD pipeline runs integration tests with verbose logging; the proxy secret leaks into CI logs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The middleware MUST NOT log the value of the shared-secret header; if ASGI access logging is enabled, the secret header must be replaced with a redacted placeholder (e.g., [REDACTED]) before the log record is written.' Add a corresponding AC: 'Unit test asserts that a request containing the shared-secret header does not produce a log record containing the secret value.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC2, replace 'valid auth_proxy_secret header' with unambiguous terminology, e.g. 'with the secret header (named by auth_proxy_secret_header, default x-proxy-auth) set to the value of the env var named by auth_proxy_secret_env'.
evidence: AC2: 'with a valid auth_proxy_secret header, the identity header yields an AccessToken'
What section defines the config keys as: 'auth_proxy_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the proxy shared secret)' and 'auth_proxy_secret_header (default x-proxy-auth)'

'auth_proxy_secret header' in AC2 is neither the config key name ('auth_proxy_secret_env') nor the header-name key ('auth_proxy_secret_header') — it's a hybrid term that appears nowhere else in the plan.
findings.finding: AC2 uses the phrase 'valid auth_proxy_secret header' which conflates the config key name ('auth_proxy_secret_env', which stores the ENV VAR NAME, not the secret itself) with the header name (configured by 'auth_proxy_secret_header'). A reader/implementer cannot unambiguously determine which entity 'auth_proxy_secret header' refers to.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2
scenarios: Implementer writes a test that checks for a header named 'auth_proxy_secret' rather than the header named by 'auth_proxy_secret_header' (default 'x-proxy-auth').
findings.suggested_fix: AC2: change 'with a valid auth_proxy_secret header' to 'with the configured secret header (auth_proxy_secret_header, default x-proxy-auth) containing the correct secret (from the env var named by auth_proxy_secret_env)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC5, replace 'once the required scope is granted' with 'once auth_proxy_scopes is configured to include the required scope' to match the static-config model described in What.
evidence: AC5: '403 with empty proxy scopes vs 200 once the required scope is granted'
What: 'auth_proxy_scopes (comma-separated fixed scope set granted to proxy-authenticated principals; OPTIONAL, defaults to an EMPTY set)'
The word 'fixed' in What contradicts the 'once the required scope is granted' dynamic implication in AC5.
findings.finding: AC5's observable oracle says 'vs 200 once the required scope is granted' — but the described feature uses a *static* scope set (auth_proxy_scopes is a flat config value), not a dynamic grant mechanism. The word 'granted' implies a runtime grant that doesn't exist.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5
scenarios: Implementer looks for a scope-grant API or mechanism that doesn't exist; or writes an incomplete test that doesn't exercise the static-config path properly.
findings.suggested_fix: AC5 observable oracle: '403 with empty auth_proxy_scopes vs 200 once auth_proxy_scopes is set to include the required scope'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md / design-doc section capturing: (a) the decision to route proxy identity through the composite rather than bypass it; (b) the header-strip-then-repopulate strategy and why it defeats spoofing; (c) the operator prerequisite (proxy MUST overwrite client-supplied forwarded headers) and where that operator-facing warning lives (e.g. README security section).
evidence: 'Docs regenerated by S7' — S7 covers config/env-var docs only, not architectural decisions
ABSENCE: no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry mentioned anywhere in the plan
'route the result through the composite (not around it)' — an explicit architectural choice with no captured rationale artifact
The security rationale for the header-stripping strategy (citing CVE references) exists inline in the plan but will not survive in any persistent doc
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel, security-critical architectural decision (trusted-proxy pattern: header-stripping middleware + proxy identity routed through the composite audience check rather than around it) but specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture the decision and its rationale. S7 only covers env-var/config doc regeneration, not architectural decision records.
findings.location: ## Approach & rationale, ## Security, ## Acceptance Criteria (AC1 — 'Docs regenerated by S7')
scenarios: A future developer changes the composite routing without realising the security invariant it enforces (audience check bypass)
A security reviewer audits the codebase and cannot find the rationale for why proxy identity is stripped-then-repopulated rather than passed through directly
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0NNN-trusted-proxy-verifier.md` (or equivalent) covering: decision, context (CVE references already in the plan), the composite-routing rationale, and the operator deployment prerequisite. Reference it from the operator/deployment docs so it is discoverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: specify that failed secret comparisons and header-stripping events are logged at WARN/ERROR level (with no secret value in the log), so operators can detect spoofing attempts and misconfiguration; add a corresponding AC asserting this log output.
evidence: Plan mentions startup failure raises 'a clear message' but says nothing about runtime logging of invalid-secret attempts or stripped-header events
No AC line requires any log/metric/trace for runtime auth failures
The stripping of X-Forwarded-* on every unauthenticated request is a high-value security signal with no specified instrumentation
findings.finding: The plan specifies no runtime observability (logging/metrics) for security-relevant events: failed shared-secret comparisons, header-stripping events, and proxy-auth denials. Operators cannot detect spoofing attempts, misconfigured proxies, or brute-force attacks on the secret without this instrumentation.
findings.location: Security section; AC — no observability AC present
scenarios: A misconfigured proxy sends requests with wrong secret — all silently denied with no operator-visible signal
An attacker probes the secret — no rate-limit or log signal to detect it
A header-smuggling attempt is silently stripped — operator never knows it was tried
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Unit/integration test asserts that a request with an invalid or missing proxy secret produces a WARN-level log entry (with no secret value) and that a stripped X-Forwarded-* header produces a DEBUG/WARN entry; no log entry is produced for valid authenticated requests (to avoid noise).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `auth_proxy_secret_env` (name-of-env-var indirection) is required over a fixed env-var name like `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET` — if no operator requirement mandates a configurable env-var name, adopt the existing direct-read pattern used by `REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD` and `WEBHOOK_TOKEN` to reduce config surface and stay consistent.
evidence: Plan: 'auth_proxy_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the proxy shared secret — no plaintext secret in config)'
opcert_service/config.py:46: `guard=_str_env('REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD')` — secret read directly from a fixed env-var name
review_bot/config.py:104: `webhook_token = os.environ.get('WEBHOOK_TOKEN', '').strip()` — secret read directly from a fixed env-var name
No existing call-site in the codebase stores an env-var NAME in config to be resolved at runtime
findings.finding: The `auth_proxy_secret_env` config key introduces a novel double-indirection pattern (config holds the NAME of an env var, which holds the secret) not present anywhere else in the codebase. All existing shared-secret patterns read from a known, fixed env-var name directly (e.g., `REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD` read directly in `opcert_service/config.py:46`; `WEBHOOK_TOKEN` read directly in `review_bot/config.py:104`). The added indirection increases config surface (two config keys where one env var would do) and departs from the codebase's established convention without a cited requirement that operators must choose their own env var names.
findings.location: What section — `auth_proxy_secret_env` config key; Security section — 'The env var named by `auth_proxy_secret_env` MUST be present'; compared to `src/rebar/opcert_service/config.py:46` and `src/rebar/review_bot/config.py:104`
scenarios: Operator must configure both `auth_proxy_secret_env` (the config key) AND the referenced env var, doubling the configuration surface vs. a fixed env-var name like `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET`.

The indirect pattern creates a new failure mode: the env-var name is misspelled in config, the secret env var is simply missing, and the error message must explain the two-level indirection — more complex than the existing pattern's 'set REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD' instruction.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `auth_proxy_secret_env` with a fixed env-var `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET` read directly (matching `opcert_service/config.py`'s `_str_env('REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD')` pattern). This removes one config key and one failure mode while preserving the 'no plaintext in config' property.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` (and its `docs/mcp-reference.md` drift gate) as a consumer of `MCP_ENV_VARS` that must be regenerated in S5 itself (or clarify how the CI drift gate is satisfied before S7 lands).
evidence: scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py:118: `from rebar.mcp_server import MCP_ENV_VARS`
scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py:120: `return {v['name']: v['description'] for v in MCP_ENV_VARS}`
.github/workflows/test.yml:308: `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py`
Plan AC: 'Docs regenerated by S7' — but the CI drift gate runs on every push, not just at S7
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` is an unenumerated consumer of `MCP_ENV_VARS`: it pulls the env-var table directly from that tuple and its `--check` drift gate runs in CI (`test.yml:308`, `gerrit-verify.yaml:262`). Adding new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_*` entries to `MCP_ENV_VARS` will cause `docs/mcp-reference.md` to go stale, failing the CI drift gate. The plan defers doc regeneration to S7 without naming this script or its drift gate as a dependency.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet; 'Docs regenerated by S7'
scenarios: S5 lands with new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_*` entries in `MCP_ENV_VARS` but `docs/mcp-reference.md` is not regenerated; CI drift gate fails with `::error::` on the PR.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` to the S5 implementation steps, or note that `make test` will catch the drift and the author must regenerate before the PR merges.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` (`build_server` and/or `main`) as an edit target in the plan's Scope/AC, and specify the ASGI wrapping seam (e.g. wrap `mcp.asgi_app()` or inject via FastMCP middleware hook).
evidence: src/rebar/mcp_server.py:240-269: `build_server()` returns a bare `FastMCP` instance with no middleware hook
src/rebar/mcp_server.py:314: `build_server().run()` in `main()`
Plan 'What': 'The middleware runs before the MCP app' — implies wrapping the ASGI app returned by build_server
ABSENCE: `mcp_server.py` `build_server`/`main` not listed as edit targets in Scope or AC
findings.finding: The plan names `mcp_server.py`'s `build_server` / `main` as an implicit edit target (ASGI middleware must wrap the app 'before the MCP app') but does not enumerate it explicitly. The current `build_server()` returns a bare `FastMCP` instance and `main()` calls `build_server().run()` — adding proxy middleware requires modifying one or both of these functions, but neither is listed as an edit target in the plan.
findings.location: What section — 'The middleware runs before the MCP app'; Scope section
scenarios: Implementer creates `_mcp_auth.py` but has no guidance on where to inject the middleware into the existing `build_server`/`main` call chain, leading to an integration gap.
If FastMCP exposes an ASGI app property, `build_server` may need to wrap it; if not, `main()` needs updating — both are unenumerated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether uvicorn's ProxyHeadersMiddleware or Starlette's TrustedHostMiddleware can be composed with a thin secret-validation layer instead of writing an entirely bespoke stripping middleware; document why they are insufficient if building custom.
evidence: uvicorn.middleware.proxy_headers.ProxyHeadersMiddleware EXISTS in the installed environment (confirmed via resolve_symbol)
starlette.middleware.trustedhost.TrustedHostMiddleware EXISTS in the installed environment (confirmed via resolve_symbol)
Plan makes no mention of these existing middleware options or why they are insufficient
findings.finding: The plan builds a bespoke ASGI header-stripping middleware but does not consider whether the already-installed `uvicorn.middleware.proxy_headers.ProxyHeadersMiddleware` (confirmed present in the repo's venv) or `starlette.middleware.trustedhost.TrustedHostMiddleware` could satisfy part of the requirement, or explain why they are insufficient prior art for this use case.
findings.location: What section — 'The middleware runs before the MCP app: it validates the shared-secret header (constant-time) and, only if valid, extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal; otherwise it strips it.'
scenarios: The custom middleware duplicates or contradicts behavior already provided by ProxyHeadersMiddleware, creating a subtle interaction bug (e.g. double-processing of X-Forwarded-For).
A simpler composition of existing middleware + a thin secret-validation layer would achieve the same security guarantee with less custom code surface area.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and specify the exact ASGI-level byte normalization strategy for header names (dash↔underscore, case folding) in the middleware design — confirm whether the approach iterates over scope['headers'] byte tuples and applies `name.lower().replace(b'_', b'-')` normalization, and that this is the correct canonical form accepted by the downstream ASGI app.
evidence: ASGI spec (PEP 3333 / ASGI spec): headers in scope['headers'] are (name_bytes, value_bytes) tuples with raw byte names
In ASGI, b'x-forwarded-user' != b'x_forwarded_user' — they are byte-distinct and require explicit normalization
Plan states normalization 'defeats underscore/case smuggling' but does not specify how it will enumerate or match the underscore variants at the ASGI byte level
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'header-name normalization defeats underscore/case smuggling' as a capability of the bespoke middleware, but in ASGI the scope['headers'] list contains raw byte tuples where b'x-forwarded-user' and b'x_forwarded_user' are byte-distinct. The plan does not verify that its normalization approach actually handles the ASGI byte-level distinction between dash and underscore variants, which is a load-bearing security claim.
findings.location: What section — 'It strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family (and the configured identity/secret headers) from any inbound request that does not present the valid secret, normalizing header-name case and underscore/dash variants to defeat smuggling.'
scenarios: An attacker sends 'X_Forwarded_User: evil' (underscore form); the bespoke middleware only strips 'x-forwarded-user' (dash form) because the normalization logic is incomplete at the byte level, allowing the header to pass through unsanitized.

AC line 'Unit test asserts a client cannot spoof identity: sending x-forwarded-user (or the underscore/case variants X_Forwarded_User, X-FORWARDED-USER) directly WITHOUT the valid secret is stripped' implies this must work — but the implementation path is unspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: In the middleware design note, specify: 'Normalize header names by decoding as latin-1, lower-casing, and replacing underscores with dashes before matching, consistent with how HTTP/2 and most ASGI frameworks canonicalize header names.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Security section declaration for the proxy-to-rebar transport leg: state that non-loopback proxy-to-rebar traffic MUST use TLS (or an equivalent private-network control), and that the 'TLS-at-edge ack' in S1 covers only the client-facing bind, not this inter-service channel — either add a new transport requirement or explicitly document that the shared-secret pattern requires the proxy and rebar to co-reside on the same host/loopback.
evidence: Plan text: 'Loopback bind is the intended deployment (S1 default); a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack) — no new bind key.'
S1's gate governs the client-to-rebar bind; it does not automatically cover the separate-host proxy-to-rebar network path.
ABSENCE: no TLS, mTLS, or network-isolation requirement on the proxy→rebar channel in the Security section or Acceptance Criteria.
In Kubernetes/ECS deployments, sidecar or separate-pod proxy patterns are common — the proxy and backend may communicate over the pod network or a service mesh, not loopback.
findings.finding: The plan delegates all non-loopback transport security to S1's existing gate, but S1's gate governs the client-facing bind address — not the proxy-to-rebar channel. In a deployment where the edge proxy and the rebar backend run on separate hosts (a common cloud/Kubernetes pattern), the shared secret and identity headers traverse an inter-host network segment. The plan does not declare a transport security requirement (TLS, mTLS, or private-network guarantee) for this proxy-to-rebar leg, leaving a plaintext-secret-over-network gap undeclared.
findings.location: Security section — 'Loopback bind is the intended deployment (S1 default); a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate'
scenarios: Operator deploys rebar behind an ALB/oauth2-proxy where the proxy runs in a separate pod or host. The proxy-to-rebar connection is plain HTTP within the cluster. An attacker with network access to the cluster can capture the shared secret from traffic and craft proxy-authenticated requests.
An operator reads the plan's 'no new bind key' and assumes S1 covers transport security end-to-end, but S1 only gates the bind address — they deploy without TLS on the inter-service leg.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, add: 'When rebar and the fronting proxy run on separate hosts, the proxy-to-rebar channel MUST be TLS-protected (or the path MUST be a private-network segment with no cross-tenant reachability); the shared secret MUST NOT transit a plaintext inter-host connection. The S1 TLS-at-edge ack covers the client bind only — operators must separately attest the inter-service transport.' Optionally add an AC: 'Documentation (S7) explicitly calls out the proxy-to-rebar transport requirement for non-loopback deployments.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (or cross-reference with a stable pointer) the full `mcp.auth_strategies` valid-value vocabulary — including the S2-introduced peer tokens (e.g. `static`, `bearer`) — so the `McpConfig` coercer and integration tests can be written without consulting S2.
evidence: Plan text: 'activated by including `proxy` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key **introduced by S2**'

AC integration test line: 'with `auth_strategies=proxy,static`, a request with no proxy identity but a valid static token still succeeds' — uses `static` as a peer strategy token, but the valid set is never enumerated in this plan
No `auth_strategies` field found in `McpConfig` or `_SECTIONS['mcp']` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` — the full vocabulary is wholly deferred
No stable cross-reference pointer (e.g. ticket ID, file path, doc URL) to the S2 spec where the enum is defined
findings.finding: The `mcp.auth_strategies` enum's full value vocabulary is never defined in this plan — only `proxy` is named as the new token; the complete set of valid strategy values (e.g. `static`, `bearer`, `oidc`) that the composite framework accepts is entirely deferred to S2 with no cross-reference pointer or stable URL. An implementor writing the `McpConfig` coercer, the AC unit tests, or the integration test (`auth_strategies=proxy,static`) must infer the vocabulary from a sibling story they may not have access to.
findings.location: What section: '`proxy` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key introduced by S2')'; AC integration test: 'auth_strategies=proxy,static'
scenarios: An implementor adding the `auth_strategies` coercer to `_SECTIONS['mcp']` needs to know the full valid set to write `_as_choice(v, k, {???})` or reject invalid values
A test author writing the `proxy,static` integration test needs to know whether `static` is the canonical token name for the static-token verifier from S2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line table to the 'What' section: '`auth_strategies` accepts a comma-separated list of `{proxy, bearer, static}` (S2 defines `bearer` and `static`; S5 adds `proxy`)' and reference the S2 story/ticket ID.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate or cross-reference (with a stable pointer to S2) the full `AccessToken` field contract and the `TokenVerifier` interface signature that the proxy verifier must satisfy.
evidence: Plan text: 'a `TokenVerifier`-shaped component so that proxy identity flows through the **same composite**'
Plan text: 'an `AccessToken` with `client_id`=the identity, `scopes`=`auth_proxy_scopes`, and `resource=auth_resource_server_url`'
No `TokenVerifier` or `AccessToken` types found in `src/rebar/` — both are S2-introduced and not co-located
The AC unit test asserts specific fields (`client_id`, `scopes`, `resource`) but their types/constraints are not defined in this plan
findings.finding: The `AccessToken` schema's full field contract — required fields, their types, and which fields are optional — is referenced inline ('`client_id`=the identity, `scopes`=`auth_proxy_scopes`, `resource=auth_resource_server_url`') but never defined or co-located in this plan; it is implicitly imported from S2. The `TokenVerifier`-shaped interface the proxy verifier must conform to is similarly undefined here. An implementor cannot verify conformance without the S2 spec.
findings.location: What section: 'the verifier turns that verified request-scoped identity into an `AccessToken` with `client_id`=the identity, `scopes`=`auth_proxy_scopes`, and `resource=auth_resource_server_url`'
scenarios: If `AccessToken` has additional required fields beyond the three named (e.g. `issued_at`, `subject`), the proxy verifier will fail type-checks at runtime
If `TokenVerifier` has a required method signature beyond `verify(request) -> AccessToken | None`, the proxy verifier will not satisfy the composite's interface

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
